parts.homeLangDetails: Here for- intensifies the root rather than indicating 'away' or 'amiss'. It turns 'stand' into a deeper, more complete act of comprehension.
From stå (to stand). In this context it echoes the idea of 'understanding' — literally standing firmly with something in mind, the same intuition behind English 'understand'.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Vi har en god forståelse for hinanden.
words.exampleTranslation: We have a good understanding of each other.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns many nouns and adjectives into transitive verbs, much like English "be-" in "befriend" or "bewilder." It is extremely common in everyday Danish and usually adds a sense of affecting or covering something. Examples: betale (pay), beskrive (describe), begynde (begin).

The core root for all things work-related, recognizable once you know it. It connects a whole cluster of everyday words and is worth memorising early. Examples: arbejde (work/job), arbejder (worker), samarbejde (cooperation).
The bare -e ending marks the infinitive in Danish, equivalent to English 'to'.

words.translationLong: to process, to work on, to rework, to elaborate
words.exampleSentence: Han skal bearbejde sine følelser efter bruddet.
words.exampleTranslation: He needs to process his feelings after the breakup.

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).
From fordre (to demand, to require). The same root appears in krav (demand) contexts — here 'ud' + 'fordre' literally means 'to call out', as in challenging someone to step up.
The -ring form is a variant of the Danish action-noun suffix -ing, used when the verb stem ends in certain consonant clusters. It functions identically to -ing.

words.exampleSentence: Det er en stor udfordring at lære et nyt sprog.
words.exampleTranslation: It is a big challenge to learn a new language.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).
From samle (to gather, to collect). Related to sam- (together) — samle is essentially 'to bring together'. The -e drops before the -ing suffix.

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

words.exampleSentence: Skolen holdt en indsamling til de fattige.
words.exampleTranslation: The school held a collection for the poor.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works exactly like English "over-" to mean above, across, or excessive. Because the mapping is so clean, guessing the meaning of new over- words is usually reliable. Examples: oversætte (translate, lit. "over-set"), overraske (surprise), overholde (comply with).
From ordne (to order, to arrange), related to orden (order). The -net ending is the past participle form — so overordnet literally means 'ordered above', i.e. placed at a higher level.
The -net ending is the Danish past participle suffix for certain verbs, equivalent to English '-ed' in 'arranged' or 'ordered'. It gives the word an adjectival sense: 'placed above'.

words.exampleSentence: Hvad er det overordnede mål med projektet?
words.exampleTranslation: What is the overarching goal of the project?

parts.homeLangDetails: Parallel to English "under-" in almost every sense, including hierarchy and insufficiency. A great cheat code for learners because intuition from English transfers directly. Examples: undervise (teach), understrege (underline), undgå (avoid, though slightly irregular).

From skrive (to write). The same root appears in skrift (writing, script) and håndskrift (handwriting). Underskrive literally means 'to write below' — the same logic as English 'subscribe' (sub- + scribere).

words.exampleSentence: Kan du underskrive kontrakten i dag?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you sign the contract today?

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in form and meaning to English "mis-," signalling that something is done incorrectly or badly. This is one of the easiest Danish prefixes for English speakers. Examples: misforstå (misunderstand), mislykkes (fail), mistro (distrust).
The same intensifying for- seen in forståelse — here it's simply part of the base word forståelse (understanding) that mis- is attached to.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Det var en total misforståelse fra min side.
words.exampleTranslation: It was a total misunderstanding on my part.
words.flag: 4 parts — borderline, but each is a meaningful morpheme in its own right

parts.homeLangDetails: Related to English "same" and German "zusammen," this prefix bundles things or people together. It is the Danish go-to equivalent of English "co-" or "together." Examples: samarbejde (cooperation), samtale (conversation), samle (collect/gather).
Here arbejde functions as a noun (work, labor) rather than a verb stem. Samarbejde is a compound of sam- (together) + arbejde (work), meaning collective effort.

words.exampleSentence: Et godt samarbejde er vigtigt på arbejdspladsen.
words.exampleTranslation: Good cooperation is important in the workplace.

parts.homeLangDetails: Carries two related meanings: clarity and readiness, similar to how English "clear" can mean both obvious and prepared. Examples: klar (clear/ready), forklare (explain), afklare (clarify).
The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Vi har brug for mere klarhed om reglerne.
words.exampleTranslation: We need more clarity about the rules.

parts.homeLangDetails: The central root for time-related vocabulary, cognate with English "tide" in its original sense of "time" (as in "yuletide"). Examples: tid (time), tidlig (early), nutid (present tense, lit. "now-time").

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).
From skrift (writing, script), related to skrive (to write). Tidsskrift literally means 'time-writing' — a periodical, something written at regular time intervals.

words.exampleSentence: Artiklen blev offentliggjort i et videnskabeligt tidsskrift.
words.exampleTranslation: The article was published in a scientific journal.

parts.homeLangDetails: Related to stående (standing) and the concept of an upright, stable position. The root stænd- here carries the sense of 'footing' or 'standing ground' — someone who stands on their own.
The Danish suffix -ig forms adjectives from nouns or roots, similar to English -y or -ish. Here it turns the noun-like concept of 'self-standing' into an adjective.

words.exampleSentence: Hun er selvstændig og driver sin egen virksomhed.
words.exampleTranslation: She is self-employed and runs her own business.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here be- turns the root into a transitive verb bekymre (to worry, to concern someone). The -ing suffix then nominalizes it back into a thing — a concern or worry.
From the root kymre, related to older senses of trouble or distress. The root is not commonly found on its own in modern Danish, but bekymre (to worry) is the standard verb.

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

words.exampleSentence: Jeg har en bekymring om hendes helbred.
words.exampleTranslation: I have a concern about her health.

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).

From forske (to research, to investigate). Related to forsker (researcher) and forskning (research). Udforskning goes further — actively going out to explore unknown territory, not just studying existing knowledge.
A variant of the -ing action-noun suffix used after certain verb stems. Functions identically to -ing.

words.exampleSentence: Udforskning af verdensrummet er fascinerende.
words.exampleTranslation: Exploration of outer space is fascinating.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).
From lære (to learn, to teach). Danish lære covers both directions — learning and teaching — depending on context. Indlæring focuses on taking knowledge in, so 'learning' is the sense here.

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

words.exampleSentence: Musik kan støtte børns indlæring.
words.exampleTranslation: Music can support children's learning.

parts.homeLangDetails: A preposition/prefix meaning 'through'. It appears in many compounds: gennemføre (to carry through), gennemse (to look through), gennemsnitlig (average, literally 'through-cut'). Here it pairs with gang (walk/passage).
From gå (to go/walk), gang refers to a going, a passage, or a corridor. Gennemgang is literally 'a going-through' — a walkthrough, review, or passageway depending on context.

words.exampleSentence: Læreren lavede en grundig gennemgang af stoffet.
words.exampleTranslation: The teacher did a thorough review of the material.

parts.homeLangDetails: From fælle (fellow, peer), related to the idea of things held in common. It appears in words like fælles (joint, mutual) and fællesskab itself.

Comparable to English "-ship" or "-hood," it forms nouns denoting a state, relationship, or collective. The family resemblance to "ship" (as in "friendship") is real and helpful. Examples: venskab (friendship), samfund (society, though compound), landskab (landscape).

words.exampleSentence: Vi har et stærkt fællesskab i vores nabolag.
words.exampleTranslation: We have a strong community in our neighborhood.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns many nouns and adjectives into transitive verbs, much like English "be-" in "befriend" or "bewilder." It is extremely common in everyday Danish and usually adds a sense of affecting or covering something. Examples: betale (pay), beskrive (describe), begynde (begin).
From vide (to know), past participial stem. Related to vid- (knowledge, awareness), as in viden (knowledge). The -st form gives the sense of 'that which is known' or 'awareness.'
The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Han havde fuld bevidsthed om sine handlinger.
words.exampleTranslation: He had full awareness of his actions.

parts.homeLangDetails: One of the busiest prefixes in Danish, it can signal doing something wrong, doing it away, or simply intensifying, depending on the verb. English has a distant cousin in "fore-" and "for-" in words like "forbid." Examples: forstå (understand), forlade (leave/abandon), forbyde (forbid).
Carries two related meanings: clarity and readiness, similar to how English "clear" can mean both obvious and prepared. Examples: klar (clear/ready), forklare (explain), afklare (clarify).

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

words.exampleSentence: Kan du give mig en forklaring på det?
words.exampleTranslation: Can you give me an explanation for that?

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Jeg så udsendelsen om klimaforandringer i aftes.
words.exampleTranslation: I watched the broadcast about climate change last night.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).

Wait — let me correct this. The root is flyd- (to flow, from flyde), with -else making it an abstract noun. The full word literally means 'a flowing in' — influence as something that flows inward and affects things, parallel to the Latin root of English 'influence' (influere = to flow in).

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Hun har stor indflydelse på sine kolleger.
words.exampleTranslation: She has great influence over her colleagues.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works exactly like English "over-" to mean above, across, or excessive. Because the mapping is so clean, guessing the meaning of new over- words is usually reliable. Examples: oversætte (translate, lit. "over-set"), overraske (surprise), overholde (comply with).
From belaste (to burden, to load), itself from be- + last (load, burden). The core idea is placing a load on something or someone.
A variant of the -ing suffix used here after the verb stem belastning. Functions identically to -ing as an action/process nominalizer.

words.exampleSentence: Overbelastning på arbejdet kan føre til stress.
words.exampleTranslation: Overload at work can lead to stress.

parts.homeLangDetails: Parallel to English "under-" in almost every sense, including hierarchy and insufficiency. A great cheat code for learners because intuition from English transfers directly. Examples: undervise (teach), understrege (underline), undgå (avoid, though slightly irregular).
From vise (to show, to point, to guide). The idea is of guiding or directing someone — teaching as showing the way. Related to vis (wise) and vejviser (signpost, guide).
A variant of -ing used after verb stems ending in a consonant. Functions identically: it turns the verb into an action or process noun.

words.exampleSentence: Undervisningen starter klokken otte om morgenen.
words.exampleTranslation: Teaching starts at eight in the morning.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in form and meaning to English "mis-," signalling that something is done incorrectly or badly. This is one of the easiest Danish prefixes for English speakers. Examples: misforstå (misunderstand), mislykkes (fail), mistro (distrust).
From vise (to show, to point). Misvise means to point in the wrong direction or mislead — literally 'to show wrongly.'

The Danish present participle suffix, equivalent to English '-ing' when used as an adjective (e.g., misleading, confusing). It turns a verb into an adjectival form describing an ongoing action or quality.

words.exampleSentence: Den misvisende information forvirrede mange mennesker.
words.exampleTranslation: The misleading information confused many people.

parts.homeLangDetails: A fuller form of sam-, the prefix meaning 'together/co-.' While the glossary lists sam- as the combining form, the free adverb sammen (together) is used in compounds like this one. Both share the same root and meaning.
From ligne (to resemble, to be like), related to lig (like, similar). The root idea is likeness — to compare is to see how things resemble each other.

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

words.exampleSentence: En sammenligning af de to produkter viser store forskelle.
words.exampleTranslation: A comparison of the two products shows major differences.

parts.homeLangDetails: The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Hun viste stor godhed over for de fremmede.
words.exampleTranslation: She showed great kindness toward the strangers.

parts.homeLangDetails: The central root for time-related vocabulary, cognate with English "tide" in its original sense of "time" (as in "yuletide"). Examples: tid (time), tidlig (early), nutid (present tense, lit. "now-time").

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).

words.exampleSentence: Vi skal aftale et tidspunkt for mødet.
words.exampleTranslation: We need to agree on a time for the meeting.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns many nouns and adjectives into transitive verbs, much like English "be-" in "befriend" or "bewilder." It is extremely common in everyday Danish and usually adds a sense of affecting or covering something. Examples: betale (pay), beskrive (describe), begynde (begin).

From ligge (to lie, to be located). The be- prefix here doesn't add transitivity in the usual sense but helps form the verbal base beligge, meaning 'to be situated.' The root ligge is the same as in English 'lie' (to lie down or be in a place).
The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Hotellets beliggenhed er perfekt — lige ved stranden.
words.exampleTranslation: The hotel's location is perfect — right by the beach.

parts.homeLangDetails: From følge (to follow, or a consequence/result). The compound selvfølge literally means 'self-following' — something that follows by itself, i.e., a matter of course. The same root appears in følgelig (consequently) and følge (to follow).

Related to English "-ly" and "-like," this suffix creates adjectives meaning "in the manner of" or "having the nature of." Many -lig adjectives also become adverbs unchanged. Examples: naturlig (natural), daglig (daily), mulig (possible).

words.exampleSentence: Selvfølgelig kan du låne min cykel.
words.exampleTranslation: Of course you can borrow my bike.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns many nouns and adjectives into transitive verbs, much like English "be-" in "befriend" or "bewilder." It is extremely common in everyday Danish and usually adds a sense of affecting or covering something. Examples: betale (pay), beskrive (describe), begynde (begin).

From rette or berettige — the root ret means 'right' (correctness, entitlement). Beretne means 'to report' or 'to give an account,' carrying the sense of an authoritative, justified telling. The connection to 'right' reflects the idea of a sanctioned or proper account.
A variant of -ing used after verb stems. Functions identically: it nominalizes the verb into an action or process noun.

words.exampleSentence: Han gav en detaljeret beretning om sine oplevelser.
words.exampleTranslation: He gave a detailed account of his experiences.

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).

From stille (to place, to set, to put). The core idea is placing or setting something out — hence displaying it. The same root appears in forestille (to imagine, literally 'to place before') and opstille (to set up).

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

words.exampleSentence: Vi besøgte en spændende udstilling på kunstmuseet.
words.exampleTranslation: We visited an exciting exhibition at the art museum.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).

words.exampleSentence: Vi mødes ved indkøbscenteret klokken to.
words.exampleTranslation: We are meeting at the shopping center at two o'clock.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here om- intensifies the idea of caring — attending to something on all sides, leaving nothing out. It contributes a sense of thoroughness rather than literal direction.
Related to hygge (comfort, coziness) and the idea of tending carefully to something. The root conveys attentiveness and gentle thoroughness.
A combined adjectival suffix (-e- + -lig) meaning '-like' or '-ly', turning the stem into an adjective. Equivalent to the standalone -lig suffix in the glossary.

words.exampleSentence: Hun er meget omhyggelig med sit arbejde.
words.exampleTranslation: She is very careful with her work.

parts.homeLangDetails: The root of betale (to pay). Related to the idea of settling a sum owed.

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).

words.exampleSentence: Kan jeg betale med betalingskort?
words.exampleTranslation: Can I pay with a payment card?

parts.homeLangDetails: In this word, for- carries the sense of doing something again or making something new again — closer to English 're-' (renew) than to its more common Danish sense of 'away' or 'amiss'. Think of it as intensifying the root toward a fresh start.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Fornyelsen af mit pas tog tre uger.
words.exampleTranslation: The renewal of my passport took three weeks.

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).
The root of bygge (to build). The same root appears in bygning (building) and bebyggelse (development/settlement).
A variant of the -ing suffix used to form action nouns, especially after verb stems ending in a consonant. Equivalent to English '-ing' as a nominalization, as in 'building' or 'expansion'.

words.exampleSentence: Udbygningen af motorvejen er planlagt til næste år.
words.exampleTranslation: The expansion of the motorway is planned for next year.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).

From lys (light). The idea is of light shining in — something that illuminates itself, making it immediately apparent. The same root appears in lyse (to shine) and oplysning (information, enlightenment).
The Danish present participle suffix, equivalent to English '-ing'. Here it turns the compound verb indlyse into an adjective meaning 'self-illuminating' — and by extension, obvious or evident.

words.exampleSentence: Det er indlysende, at han har ret.
words.exampleTranslation: It is obvious that he is right.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here over- doesn't mean 'too much' but rather 'upon' or 'catching from above' — as in taking someone unawares. The same sense appears in overfalde (to assault, literally 'fall upon').
Related to rask (quick, brisk, healthy). The verb overraske means to come upon someone quickly, to catch them off guard. The root evokes suddenness and speed.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Det var en stor overraskelse at se ham der.
words.exampleTranslation: It was a big surprise to see him there.

parts.homeLangDetails: Parallel to English "under-" in almost every sense, including hierarchy and insufficiency. A great cheat code for learners because intuition from English transfers directly. Examples: undervise (teach), understrege (underline), undgå (avoid, though slightly irregular).
The stem of vurdere (to assess, to evaluate, to appraise). Related to værd (worth) and vurdering (assessment). The same root appears in overvurdere (to overestimate).
Danish infinitives typically end in -e. This is the infinitive marker, equivalent to English 'to'.

words.translationLong: to underestimate, to undervalue, to underrate
words.exampleSentence: Du må ikke undervurdere hans evner.
words.exampleTranslation: You must not underestimate his abilities.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in form and meaning to English "mis-," signalling that something is done incorrectly or badly. This is one of the easiest Danish prefixes for English speakers. Examples: misforstå (misunderstand), mislykkes (fail), mistro (distrust).
The stem of bruge (to use). Related to brug (use) and bruger (user). The same root appears in misbrug (abuse/misuse as a noun).
Danish infinitives typically end in -e. This is the infinitive marker.

words.translationLong: to misuse, to abuse, to exploit
words.exampleSentence: Det er forbudt at misbruge sin magt.
words.exampleTranslation: It is forbidden to abuse one's power.

parts.homeLangDetails: An expanded form of sam- (together/co-), here as the full word sammen (together). It sets the core idea of people coming together.
A noun derived from komme (to come). It appears in other compounds like ankomst (arrival) and tilkomst (access). Here it captures the act of people coming together in one place.

words.exampleSentence: Vi holder en lille sammenkomst fredag aften.
words.exampleTranslation: We are having a small get-together on Friday evening.

parts.homeLangDetails: Carries two related meanings: clarity and readiness, similar to how English "clear" can mean both obvious and prepared. Examples: klar (clear/ready), forklare (explain), afklare (clarify).
The stem of lægge (to lay, to place). Common in Danish compounds: lægge vægt på (to emphasize), fremlægge (to present). Here it gives the sense of laying something out clearly for inspection.
Part of the infinitive ending -ge from lægge. The full infinitive form after combining klar + lægge becomes klarlægge, with -ge as the infinitive tail.

words.translationLong: to clarify, to establish, to elucidate, to lay out clearly
words.exampleSentence: Vi er nødt til at klarlægge reglerne.
words.exampleTranslation: We need to clarify the rules.

parts.homeLangDetails: The central root for time-related vocabulary, cognate with English "tide" in its original sense of "time" (as in "yuletide"). Examples: tid (time), tidlig (early), nutid (present tense, lit. "now-time").

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).

A very productive Danish suffix meaning 'in terms of' or 'relating to'. It is equivalent to English '-wise' (as in timewise) or '-related'. You see it in words like indholdsmæssig (content-wise) and størrelsesmæssig (size-wise).

words.translationLong: in terms of time, time-related, temporal
words.exampleSentence: Tidsmæssigt er det svært at nå det hele.
words.exampleTranslation: Time-wise, it is difficult to get everything done.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means "place" or "location" and shows up constantly in compound words, parallel to English "-stead" as in "homestead." Examples: sted (place), hjemsted (hometown), i stedet for (instead of).
Here for- functions as a preposition meaning 'for' or 'in place of', not the prefix meaning 'away/amiss'. Together with sted (place) it builds the idea of occupying someone's place.
The stem of træde (to step, to tread). In this compound it conveys stepping into someone's place. The same root appears in indtræde (to enter/step in) and optræde (to perform/step up).
Identical in function to English "-er," naming the person or thing that performs an action. You will recognize this pattern instantly. Examples: lærer (teacher), arbejder (worker), sanger (singer).

words.exampleSentence: Hans stedfortræder vil lede mødet i dag.
words.exampleTranslation: His deputy will lead the meeting today.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here be- doesn't make a verb transitive in the usual sense, but rather forms an adjective from the root vid- (know/witness). It gives the word an intensified, participial feel — 'having been made aware'.

Related to vide (to know) and vidne (witness). The -st ending gives it a participial or superlative character. The root is cognate with English 'wit' and 'wisdom'. Together with be-, it conveys the idea of something known to oneself — conscious, deliberate.

words.exampleSentence: Hun tog en bevidst beslutning om at stoppe.
words.exampleTranslation: She made a conscious decision to stop.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here for- contributes a sense of things going off track or being held back — something slipping away from its intended timing. This is the 'amiss/away' sense of for-, as opposed to its intensive use.
Related to synke (to sink, to slow down). The idea is of something sinking behind schedule, losing pace. The verbal form forsinke means 'to delay'.
The Danish past participle suffix for neuter or adjectival use. Here it makes forsinke into the adjective/participle forsinket (delayed, late).

words.exampleSentence: Toget er forsinket med tyve minutter.
words.exampleTranslation: The train is delayed by twenty minutes.

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).
From the verb slette (to erase, to wipe out, to delete). Related to 'slet' meaning bad or plain — the core idea is reducing something to nothing.
The -t here is part of the participial stem of slette → udslettet, before the abstract noun suffix -else is added.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Krigen førte til udslettelse af hele landsbyer.
words.exampleTranslation: The war led to the annihilation of entire villages.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).
From blande (to mix, to blend). In this compound it takes on the sense of meddling — mixing yourself into something that isn't your business.

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

words.exampleSentence: Han tolererede ingen indblanding i sine private anliggender.
words.exampleTranslation: He tolerated no interference in his private affairs.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works exactly like English "over-" to mean above, across, or excessive. Because the mapping is so clean, guessing the meaning of new over- words is usually reliable. Examples: oversætte (translate, lit. "over-set"), overraske (surprise), overholde (comply with).
From gå (to go/walk), with -gang forming a noun meaning a going, a passage, or a corridor. Related to English 'gang' in its older sense of a path or course.

words.exampleSentence: Overgangen fra vinter til forår kan være langsom.
words.exampleTranslation: The transition from winter to spring can be slow.

parts.homeLangDetails: Parallel to English "under-" in almost every sense, including hierarchy and insufficiency. A great cheat code for learners because intuition from English transfers directly. Examples: undervise (teach), understrege (underline), undgå (avoid, though slightly irregular).
From lægge (to lay, to place). A lag is something laid down — a layer, a base, or a foundation. The same root appears in beliggenhed (location) and indlæg (insert).

words.exampleSentence: Sæt et underlag under koppen, så bordet ikke bliver ridset.
words.exampleTranslation: Put a mat under the cup so the table doesn't get scratched.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in form and meaning to English "mis-," signalling that something is done incorrectly or badly. This is one of the easiest Danish prefixes for English speakers. Examples: misforstå (misunderstand), mislykkes (fail), mistro (distrust).
From nøje, related to nyde (to enjoy) and tilfredshed. Nøje here carries the sense of being content or pleased — so misnøje is literally 'bad-contentment', i.e. dissatisfaction.

words.exampleSentence: Borgerne udtrykte deres misnøje med de nye regler.
words.exampleTranslation: The citizens expressed their dissatisfaction with the new rules.

parts.homeLangDetails: Related to English "same" and German "zusammen," this prefix bundles things or people together. It is the Danish go-to equivalent of English "co-" or "together." Examples: samarbejde (cooperation), samtale (conversation), samle (collect/gather).

From ligge (to lie down) — leje here is a noun meaning lying together, a bed, or a couch. The same root appears in leje (to rent, literally to arrange a place to lie) and beleje (to besiege, to lay around).

words.exampleSentence: Loven definerer samleje som en central del af seksuelle overgreb.
words.exampleTranslation: The law defines sexual intercourse as a central element of sexual assault.

parts.homeLangDetails: Carries two related meanings: clarity and readiness, similar to how English "clear" can mean both obvious and prepared. Examples: klar (clear/ready), forklare (explain), afklare (clarify).
From se (to see) — syn is the noun meaning sight, vision, or view. It appears in words like synspunkt (viewpoint) and øjensyn (appearance).

The -et ending here is the definite/neuter adjectival inflection, as in 'klarsynet' used predicatively or with a definite noun. It is not the abstract noun suffix -hed or a separate morpheme — just agreement marking.

words.exampleSentence: Hun er en klarsynet leder, der altid ser tingene som de er.
words.exampleTranslation: She is a clear-sighted leader who always sees things as they are.

parts.homeLangDetails: The central root for time-related vocabulary, cognate with English "tide" in its original sense of "time" (as in "yuletide"). Examples: tid (time), tidlig (early), nutid (present tense, lit. "now-time").

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).

words.exampleSentence: Vi er bagud i forhold til tidsplanen.
words.exampleTranslation: We are behind schedule.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means "place" or "location" and shows up constantly in compound words, parallel to English "-stead" as in "homestead." Examples: sted (place), hjemsted (hometown), i stedet for (instead of).

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).
A shortening of sans, meaning sense or faculty (as in the five senses — de fem sanser). Here it refers to an intuitive spatial or geographical faculty.

words.translationLong: sense of direction, spatial awareness, sense of place
words.exampleSentence: Han har en fremragende stedsans og finder altid vej.
words.exampleTranslation: He has an excellent sense of direction and always finds his way.

parts.homeLangDetails: Related to English "same" and German "zusammen," this prefix bundles things or people together. It is the Danish go-to equivalent of English "co-" or "together." Examples: samarbejde (cooperation), samtale (conversation), samle (collect/gather).
From finde or a Germanic root meaning foundation or ground. In samfund (society), it carries the sense of something founded together — a community built on shared ground. See also: samfund.

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).

Comparable to English "-ship" or "-hood," it forms nouns denoting a state, relationship, or collective. The family resemblance to "ship" (as in "friendship") is real and helpful. Examples: venskab (friendship), samfund (society, though compound), landskab (landscape).

words.translationLong: civic community, sense of community, social fellowship
words.exampleSentence: Skolen fremmer samfundsskab og sammenhold blandt eleverne.
words.exampleTranslation: The school promotes civic community and cohesion among the students.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here be- acts on give (to give/happen) to form begive, meaning to betake oneself or to occur. It shifts the verb toward reflexive or intensified action rather than strict transitivity.
From give (to give), but in the reflexive construction begive sig (to betake oneself, to happen). The sense here is closer to 'what comes to pass' than literal giving.
The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Det var en historisk begivenhed, som ingen vil glemme.
words.exampleTranslation: It was a historic event that no one will forget.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here for- intensifies dyb (deep) into an action of going deeply into something — it's not 'away' or 'amiss' but rather a deepening intensification, pulling you further in.
From dyb (deep). The same root appears in dybde (depth) and dybtgående (profound/deep-going). Here it forms the verb fordybe sig — to immerse oneself deeply in something.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Læsning kræver ro og fordybelse.
words.exampleTranslation: Reading requires quiet and deep absorption.

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).
From fordre (to demand, to require, to call for) — related to German fordern. The root carries the sense of calling something forth or making a claim on it.
The -re ending is the Danish infinitive suffix, the equivalent of English 'to'. It is not the Latin prefix re- (again/back) — it's simply how this verb ends in its base form.

words.translationLong: to challenge, to push, to put to the test
words.exampleSentence: Det er vigtigt at udfordre sig selv hver dag.
words.exampleTranslation: It is important to challenge yourself every day.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).

A short connector vowel inserted between elements in compounds purely for pronunciation smoothness. It has no meaning of its own but its absence can make a compound feel awkward. Examples: grøntsager (vegetables, lit. "green-e-things"), dannebrog (the Danish flag), håndværker (craftsman).
From holde (to hold). With ind- and the linking vowel, it becomes indeholde — to hold within, to contain. The same root gives indhold (content, literally 'what is held inside').
The -er here is the Danish present tense ending, not the agent suffix. Indeholder means '(it) contains', not 'a container'.

words.exampleSentence: Denne pakke indeholder skrøbelige genstande.
words.exampleTranslation: This package contains fragile items.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works exactly like English "over-" to mean above, across, or excessive. Because the mapping is so clean, guessing the meaning of new over- words is usually reliable. Examples: oversætte (translate, lit. "over-set"), overraske (surprise), overholde (comply with).
From sætte (to set, to put, to place). In oversætte, it means to carry something across — to set it over from one language to another. Related to German übersetzen, with exactly the same logic.
The -t is part of the verbal stem before the abstract noun suffix -else is added, parallel to udslettelse. It marks the verb as participial before nominalization.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Oversættelsen af romanen tog to år.
words.exampleTranslation: The translation of the novel took two years.

parts.homeLangDetails: Parallel to English "under-" in almost every sense, including hierarchy and insufficiency. A great cheat code for learners because intuition from English transfers directly. Examples: undervise (teach), understrege (underline), undgå (avoid, though slightly irregular).
From 'orden' (order). The root 'ord' here carries the sense of rank or ordering, not the word 'ord' meaning 'word'. Think of it as being placed under-order — ranked beneath something else.
The suffix -net marks a past participle in Danish, equivalent to English '-ed'. So 'underordnet' is literally 'under-ordered' or 'subordinated'.

words.exampleSentence: Han er underordnet direktøren i virksomheden.
words.exampleTranslation: He is subordinate to the director in the company.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in form and meaning to English "mis-," signalling that something is done incorrectly or badly. This is one of the easiest Danish prefixes for English speakers. Examples: misforstå (misunderstand), mislykkes (fail), mistro (distrust).
From 'lykke' (luck, happiness, success). The verb 'lykkes' means 'to succeed' — so 'mis-lykkes' is to fail at succeeding, i.e., to go wrong.
The ending -des marks the past tense of a passive or reflexive verb in Danish. 'Mislykkedes' is the past tense of 'mislykkes' (to fail).

words.translationLong: failed, did not succeed, went wrong
words.exampleSentence: Forsøget mislykkedes på grund af dårligt vejr.
words.exampleTranslation: The attempt failed due to bad weather.

parts.homeLangDetails: A fuller form of the prefix sam-, meaning 'together'. Here it appears as 'sammen' rather than the reduced prefix form.
From 'hænge' (to hang). The idea is things that hang together — that are connected or cohesive. The same root appears in 'hænge sammen' (to cohere, to hang together).

words.exampleSentence: Det er vigtigt at forstå ordet i den rette sammenhæng.
words.exampleTranslation: It is important to understand the word in the right context.

parts.homeLangDetails: From 'tro' (faith, belief, loyalty). It can mean both religious belief and personal faithfulness or loyalty.
The suffix -skab forms abstract nouns expressing a state or quality, similar to English '-ship' or '-hood'. So 'tro' + '-skab' = faithfulness, the state of being faithful.

words.exampleSentence: Troskab er grundlaget for ethvert godt venskab.
words.exampleTranslation: Faithfulness is the foundation of any good friendship.

parts.homeLangDetails: The central root for time-related vocabulary, cognate with English "tide" in its original sense of "time" (as in "yuletide"). Examples: tid (time), tidlig (early), nutid (present tense, lit. "now-time").

Related to English "-ly" and "-like," this suffix creates adjectives meaning "in the manner of" or "having the nature of." Many -lig adjectives also become adverbs unchanged. Examples: naturlig (natural), daglig (daily), mulig (possible).

words.exampleSentence: Vi stod tidlig op for at se solopgangen.
words.exampleTranslation: We got up early to see the sunrise.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means "place" or "location" and shows up constantly in compound words, parallel to English "-stead" as in "homestead." Examples: sted (place), hjemsted (hometown), i stedet for (instead of).

Related to English "-ly" and "-like," this suffix creates adjectives meaning "in the manner of" or "having the nature of." Many -lig adjectives also become adverbs unchanged. Examples: naturlig (natural), daglig (daily), mulig (possible).

words.exampleSentence: Den stedlige myndighed er ansvarlig for vejene.
words.exampleTranslation: The local authority is responsible for the roads.

parts.homeLangDetails: An independent Danish word meaning 'self' or 'oneself', used as a compounding element much like English 'self-' in 'self-awareness'.
From 'bevidst' (conscious, aware). Related to 'vide' (to know) — so 'bevidst' is something like 'be-knowledged', i.e., aware or conscious.
The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Selvbevidsthed er en vigtig egenskab for en leder.
words.exampleTranslation: Self-awareness is an important quality for a leader.
words.flag: 3 parts, but 'selvbevidsthed' could be further split (selv + be + vidst + hed); kept at 3 meaningful units for clarity

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns many nouns and adjectives into transitive verbs, much like English "be-" in "befriend" or "bewilder." It is extremely common in everyday Danish and usually adds a sense of affecting or covering something. Examples: betale (pay), beskrive (describe), begynde (begin).
The past participle of 'kende' (to know/recognize). 'Bekendt' means 'acquainted' or 'known', and 'bekendtskab' is the abstract noun built from it.

Comparable to English "-ship" or "-hood," it forms nouns denoting a state, relationship, or collective. The family resemblance to "ship" (as in "friendship") is real and helpful. Examples: venskab (friendship), samfund (society, though compound), landskab (landscape).

words.exampleSentence: Vi gjorte bekendtskab med vores nye naboer i weekenden.
words.exampleTranslation: We made the acquaintance of our new neighbors over the weekend.

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).
From 'folde' (to fold, to unfold). 'Udfolde' means to unfold or to develop — to open something out. The root 'fold' here captures unfolding rather than folding up.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Kunst giver mulighed for personlig udfoldelse.
words.exampleTranslation: Art provides an opportunity for personal expression.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).
From 'lægge' (to lay, to place). 'Indlægge' means to lay in or place inside — used for admitting someone to hospital or inserting something.
The ending -ge here is the remnant of the infinitive ending on 'lægge'. After 'ind-' is prefixed to the stem 'læg', the infinitive is reconstructed as 'indlægge'.

words.translationLong: to admit (to hospital), to insert, to embed
words.exampleSentence: Lægen besluttede at indlægge patienten til observation.
words.exampleTranslation: The doctor decided to admit the patient for observation.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works exactly like English "over-" to mean above, across, or excessive. Because the mapping is so clean, guessing the meaning of new over- words is usually reliable. Examples: oversætte (translate, lit. "over-set"), overraske (surprise), overholde (comply with).
From 'holde' (to hold, to keep). 'Overholde' means to keep or uphold — to hold something above the line, i.e., to comply with or observe a rule or deadline.

A short connector vowel inserted between elements in compounds purely for pronunciation smoothness. It has no meaning of its own but its absence can make a compound feel awkward. Examples: grøntsager (vegetables, lit. "green-e-things"), dannebrog (the Danish flag), håndværker (craftsman).

words.translationLong: to comply with, to observe, to keep (a deadline/rule)
words.exampleSentence: Det er vigtigt at overholde reglerne i trafikken.
words.exampleTranslation: It is important to observe the rules of traffic.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here 'under' doesn't mean spatially below, but temporally 'during' or 'in the course of' — a common Danish usage. 'Undervejs' literally means 'during the way', i.e., on the way.
The genitive form of 'vej' (road, way). The -s is a genitive marker, making 'undervejs' literally 'under the way's [course]' — i.e., in transit or in progress.

words.translationLong: on the way, en route, in progress, underway
words.exampleSentence: Vi er undervejs mod sommerhuset.
words.exampleTranslation: We are on the way to the summer house.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in form and meaning to English "mis-," signalling that something is done incorrectly or badly. This is one of the easiest Danish prefixes for English speakers. Examples: misforstå (misunderstand), mislykkes (fail), mistro (distrust).
From 'bruge' (to use). 'Brug' is the noun form meaning 'use' or 'usage'. So 'misbrug' is quite literally 'mis-use' or 'abuse'.

words.exampleSentence: Misbrug af alkohol kan have alvorlige helbredsmæssige konsekvenser.
words.exampleTranslation: Abuse of alcohol can have serious health consequences.

parts.homeLangDetails: Related to English "same" and German "zusammen," this prefix bundles things or people together. It is the Danish go-to equivalent of English "co-" or "together." Examples: samarbejde (cooperation), samtale (conversation), samle (collect/gather).
From 'virke' (to work, to function, to act). Related to 'virkning' (effect) and 'virksomhed' (company/activity). 'Samvirke' is working together — cooperation or collaboration.

words.exampleSentence: Projektet lykkedes takket være et godt samvirke mellem holdene.
words.exampleTranslation: The project succeeded thanks to good cooperation between the teams.

parts.homeLangDetails: Carries two related meanings: clarity and readiness, similar to how English "clear" can mean both obvious and prepared. Examples: klar (clear/ready), forklare (explain), afklare (clarify).
From 'gøre' (to make, to do). In compound verbs, it combines with adjectives to mean 'make [adjective]' — so 'klargøre' means 'to make ready', i.e., to prepare or clear.

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

words.exampleSentence: Klargøring af fly tager normalt mindst en time.
words.exampleTranslation: Preparation of an aircraft normally takes at least an hour.

parts.homeLangDetails: The central root for time-related vocabulary, cognate with English "tide" in its original sense of "time" (as in "yuletide"). Examples: tid (time), tidlig (early), nutid (present tense, lit. "now-time").

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).

words.exampleSentence: Det skete inden for et kort tidsrum.
words.exampleTranslation: It happened within a short period of time.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means "place" or "location" and shows up constantly in compound words, parallel to English "-stead" as in "homestead." Examples: sted (place), hjemsted (hometown), i stedet for (instead of).

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).

words.exampleSentence: Aarhus er et gammelt stedsnavn med nordisk oprindelse.
words.exampleTranslation: Aarhus is an old place name of Nordic origin.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns many nouns and adjectives into transitive verbs, much like English "be-" in "befriend" or "bewilder." It is extremely common in everyday Danish and usually adds a sense of affecting or covering something. Examples: betale (pay), beskrive (describe), begynde (begin).
From 'vide' (to know) — 'bevidst' means conscious or aware, and 'vidst' is its stem as it appears here in the compound.
The verb 'gøre' (to make, to do) functions here as a verbalizing element, turning the adjective 'bevidst' into a causative verb: to make (someone) aware.

words.translationLong: to raise awareness, to make conscious of
words.exampleSentence: Vi ønsker at bevidstgøre folk om klimaforandringer.
words.exampleTranslation: We want to raise people's awareness of climate change.

parts.homeLangDetails: In 'forstå' (to understand), the prefix 'for-' intensifies the base verb 'stå' (to stand), giving a sense of fully grasping or standing firmly in comprehension. It's a frozen compound rather than a transparent separation + prefix combination.
From 'stå' (to stand). Together with 'for-', it forms 'forstå' (to understand) — literally something like 'stand fully/firmly in grasp of'.
A suffix combining '-e-' (a linking vowel) and '-lig' (the adjectival suffix equivalent to English '-like' or '-ly'). Together they form '-elig', meaning 'capable of being' — like '-able' in English.

words.exampleSentence: Forklaringen var klar og forståelig for alle.
words.exampleTranslation: The explanation was clear and understandable to everyone.

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).
Related to 'sted' (place), but here used in a transferred sense of formally placing something — issuing or putting something out. The '-e' ending is the infinitive marker.

words.translationLong: to issue, to emit, to grant (officially)
words.exampleSentence: Ambassaden kan udstede et nødpas inden for 24 timer.
words.exampleTranslation: The embassy can issue an emergency passport within 24 hours.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).

This highly productive root appears in many compound and prefixed verbs, much like English "go" in phrasal verbs. Learning it unlocks a large family of words. Examples: gå (go/walk), indgå (enter into), foregå (take place).
The '-ende' suffix is the Danish present participle ending, equivalent to English '-ing'. Here it gives the sense of 'going in deeply' — thorough, in-depth.

words.exampleSentence: Hun har en indgående viden om emnet.
words.exampleTranslation: She has an in-depth knowledge of the subject.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works exactly like English "over-" to mean above, across, or excessive. Because the mapping is so clean, guessing the meaning of new over- words is usually reliable. Examples: oversætte (translate, lit. "over-set"), overraske (surprise), overholde (comply with).
From 'leve' (to live). The '-e' is dropped before the suffix, leaving the stem 'lev-'. This is the core of 'overleve' (to survive) — to live beyond or past something.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Overlevelse i ørkenen kræver viden om vandkilder.
words.exampleTranslation: Survival in the desert requires knowledge of water sources.

parts.homeLangDetails: In 'undervise' (to teach, to instruct), 'under-' carries the sense of guiding someone from below — supporting and instructing. It's a common component in Danish educational vocabulary.
From 'vise' (to show) + '-ning' (action noun suffix). 'Undervisning' means teaching or instruction — literally the act of showing/guiding from below.

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).
Means "place" or "location" and shows up constantly in compound words, parallel to English "-stead" as in "homestead." Examples: sted (place), hjemsted (hometown), i stedet for (instead of).

words.exampleSentence: Biblioteket fungerer som undervisningssted for mange studerende.
words.exampleTranslation: The library serves as a teaching venue for many students.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in form and meaning to English "mis-," signalling that something is done incorrectly or badly. This is one of the easiest Danish prefixes for English speakers. Examples: misforstå (misunderstand), mislykkes (fail), mistro (distrust).
Here part of the root 'forhold' (relationship, ratio, condition). The 'for-' in 'forhold' is a frozen prefix from 'holde' (to hold), not carrying its full independent meaning.
From 'holde' (to hold). 'Forhold' means relationship, ratio, or condition — literally something held together. With 'mis-' added, the resulting word means disproportion or disparity.

words.exampleSentence: Der er et klart misforhold mellem indsats og belønning.
words.exampleTranslation: There is a clear disproportion between effort and reward.

parts.homeLangDetails: Related to English "same" and German "zusammen," this prefix bundles things or people together. It is the Danish go-to equivalent of English "co-" or "together." Examples: samarbejde (cooperation), samtale (conversation), samle (collect/gather).
From 'høre' (to hear, but also to belong). 'Høre til' means to belong to. Here 'hør-' carries the sense of belonging, so 'samhørighed' is the quality of belonging together.

Functions like English "-y" or "-ic," converting nouns into adjectives. It is extremely common and usually signals "having the quality of." Examples: venlig (friendly), rolig (calm), farlig (dangerous).
The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Der er stor samhørighed i det lille lokalsamfund.
words.exampleTranslation: There is a great sense of belonging in the small local community.

parts.homeLangDetails: Carries two related meanings: clarity and readiness, similar to how English "clear" can mean both obvious and prepared. Examples: klar (clear/ready), forklare (explain), afklare (clarify).
The '-et' here is a participial/adjectival ending, turning 'klarsyn' (clear vision) into 'klarsynet' (clear-sighted) — an adjective describing someone who sees clearly or has sharp insight.
The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Hendes klarsynethed i krisetider var beundringsværdig.
words.exampleTranslation: Her clear-sightedness in times of crisis was admirable.

parts.homeLangDetails: The central root for time-related vocabulary, cognate with English "tide" in its original sense of "time" (as in "yuletide"). Examples: tid (time), tidlig (early), nutid (present tense, lit. "now-time").

This little connector appears between parts of compound nouns, acting as a linking element and sometimes reflecting an old genitive. English has no direct equivalent but it is easy to spot once you know it. Examples: arbejdsdag (workday), landsby (village, lit. "land-s-by"), vindueskarm (windowsill).
Part of 'forbrug' (consumption, use). The 'for-' here carries a sense of using up or consuming — something expended or used away. It's a frozen prefix in this compound.
From 'bruge' (to use). 'Forbrug' means consumption or expenditure. Together with 'tids-', it describes the expenditure of time.

words.exampleSentence: Projektet kræver et stort tidsforbrug.
words.exampleTranslation: The project requires a large expenditure of time.

parts.homeLangDetails: Means "place" or "location" and shows up constantly in compound words, parallel to English "-stead" as in "homestead." Examples: sted (place), hjemsted (hometown), i stedet for (instead of).
From 'fæste' (to fasten, to fix, to attach). Together with 'sted-', it means to fix or anchor something to a place — to locate or situate something precisely.

words.translationLong: to locate, to place, to fix in a location
words.exampleSentence: Det er svært at stedfæste begivenheden præcist.
words.exampleTranslation: It is difficult to precisely locate the event.

parts.homeLangDetails: Part of the frozen compound 'forståelse' (understanding), from 'forstå' (to understand). The 'for-' here intensifies 'stå' (to stand) in a frozen combination meaning comprehension.
From 'stå' (to stand). Together with 'for-', forms 'forstå' (to understand). The compound 'selvforståelse' literally describes the understanding one has of oneself.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Terapien hjalp ham med at udvikle en dybere selvforståelse.
words.exampleTranslation: The therapy helped him develop a deeper self-understanding.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns many nouns and adjectives into transitive verbs, much like English "be-" in "befriend" or "bewilder." It is extremely common in everyday Danish and usually adds a sense of affecting or covering something. Examples: betale (pay), beskrive (describe), begynde (begin).
From 'vare' (to last, to keep watch over) or related to 'bevare' (to preserve, to keep). The stem 'var-' here carries the sense of guarding or maintaining something over time.

Turns verbs and adjectives into abstract nouns, roughly like English "-ness" or "-ance." It is one of the most productive suffixes in formal and everyday Danish alike. Examples: følelse (feeling), forståelse (understanding), oplevelse (experience).

words.exampleSentence: Bevarelse af kulturarven er en vigtig opgave.
words.exampleTranslation: The preservation of cultural heritage is an important task.

parts.homeLangDetails: One of the busiest prefixes in Danish, it can signal doing something wrong, doing it away, or simply intensifying, depending on the verb. English has a distant cousin in "fore-" and "for-" in words like "forbid." Examples: forstå (understand), forlade (leave/abandon), forbyde (forbid).

words.exampleSentence: Det kræver meget forarbejde at starte en virksomhed.
words.exampleTranslation: Starting a business requires a lot of preliminary work.

parts.homeLangDetails: Mirrors English "out-" almost perfectly, giving a sense of outward movement or completion. You will spot it constantly in spoken Danish because Danes love phrasal-verb-style compounds. Examples: udgang (exit), udvikle (develop), udtale (pronounce).
From the verb 'ligne' (to equalize, to level out, to balance). Related to 'lig' (equal, alike). Together with 'ud-', the idea is of evening things out — bringing them to the same level.

Directly cognate with English "-ing," it forms nouns from verbs describing an action or its result. The connection makes these words very easy to absorb. Examples: læsning (reading), træning (training), udvikling (development).

words.exampleSentence: Udligningen af pointene skete i kampens sidste minut.
words.exampleTranslation: The equalization of the scores happened in the last minute of the match.

parts.homeLangDetails: The natural partner to ud-, meaning movement inward or inclusion, just like English "in-." It is often used where English would use "in-" or "into." Examples: indgang (entrance), indkøb (shopping), indflytning (move-in).
From 'samle' (to gather, to collect, to assemble). The root conveys bringing things together into one place or group.

A short connector vowel inserted between elements in compounds purely for pronunciation smoothness. It has no meaning of its own but its absence can make a compound feel awkward. Examples: grøntsager (vegetables, lit. "green-e-things"), dannebrog (the Danish flag), håndværker (craftsman).

words.translationLong: to collect, to gather, to raise (funds)
words.exampleSentence: Vi indsamler penge til velgørenhed hvert år.
words.exampleTranslation: We collect money for charity every year.

parts.homeLangDetails: Works exactly like English "over-" to mean above, across, or excessive. Because the mapping is so clean, guessing the meaning of new over- words is usually reliable. Examples: oversætte (translate, lit. "over-set"), overraske (surprise), overholde (comply with).
Rooted in 'flyde' (to flow). 'Overflødigt' literally means 'overflowing' — there is more than enough, it spills over. The same root gives 'overflod' (abundance, plenty).
The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Landet lever i overflødighed trods den globale krise.
words.exampleTranslation: The country lives in abundance despite the global crisis.

parts.homeLangDetails: Parallel to English "under-" in almost every sense, including hierarchy and insufficiency. A great cheat code for learners because intuition from English transfers directly. Examples: undervise (teach), understrege (underline), undgå (avoid, though slightly irregular).
From 'skyde' (to shoot, to push). Here the sense is of something pushed below the line — a shortfall. The same root appears in 'skud' (a shot) and 'forskyde' (to shift/displace).

words.exampleSentence: Virksomheden har et stort underskud dette kvartal.
words.exampleTranslation: The company has a large deficit this quarter.

parts.homeLangDetails: Identical in form and meaning to English "mis-," signalling that something is done incorrectly or badly. This is one of the easiest Danish prefixes for English speakers. Examples: misforstå (misunderstand), mislykkes (fail), mistro (distrust).
From 'forstå' (to understand), itself a compound of 'for-' (intensive/completive) + 'stå' (to stand). Literally 'to stand fully in something' — to grasp it completely.
The suffix '-elig' (also spelled '-lig') forms adjectives from verbs or nouns, often with the sense of '-able' or '-ible' in English. Here: capable of being misunderstood.

words.exampleSentence: Den besked var meget misforståelig og skabte forvirring.
words.exampleTranslation: That message was very easy to misunderstand and caused confusion.

parts.homeLangDetails: Related to English "same" and German "zusammen," this prefix bundles things or people together. It is the Danish go-to equivalent of English "co-" or "together." Examples: samarbejde (cooperation), samtale (conversation), samle (collect/gather).
From 'spille' (to play). In this compound it refers not to a game but to the interplay or interaction between things — the way they 'play together'.

words.exampleSentence: Der er et godt samspil mellem de to musikere.
words.exampleTranslation: There is a good interplay between the two musicians.

parts.homeLangDetails: Carries two related meanings: clarity and readiness, similar to how English "clear" can mean both obvious and prepared. Examples: klar (clear/ready), forklare (explain), afklare (clarify).
The direct cognate of English "-hood" and German "-heit," it creates abstract nouns from adjectives describing a state or quality. Examples: frihed (freedom), skønhed (beauty), sandhed (truth).

words.exampleSentence: Vi har brug for mere klarhed om planerne fremover.
words.exampleTranslation: We need more clarity about the plans going forward.

parts.homeLangDetails: This prefix turns many nouns and adjectives into transitive verbs, much like English "be-" in "befriend" or "bewilder." It is extremely common in everyday Danish and usually adds a sense of affecting or covering something. Examples: betale (pay), beskrive (describe), begynde (begin).

From 'stemme' (to determine, to define, but also to vote or to match). In compounds with 'be-', 'bestemme' means to decide or to define something. Here the idea is of something defined or set by time.
The '-t' ending here marks the past participle used as an adjective. 'Bestemt' means 'determined' or 'defined'; 'tidsbestemt' means 'time-defined' or 'time-limited'.

words.exampleSentence: Det er et tidsbestemt projekt, der slutter til sommer.
words.exampleTranslation: It is a time-limited project that ends in the summer.
